lesson.title: Errors
lesson.summary: Handle failures explicitly — Go uses returned values, not exceptions.

sections.heading: Errors are values
sections.bodyHtml:
In Go, errors are ordinary values. Functions that can fail return an error as their last return value — you check it yourself:

f, err := os.Open("file.txt")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
defer f.Close()

The error type is a built-in interface:

type error interface {
    Error() string
}

sections.heading: Creating errors
sections.bodyHtml:
Create simple errors with errors.New or fmt.Errorf:

import "errors"

var ErrNotFound = errors.New("item not found")

func find(id int) (string, error) {
    if id < 0 {
        return "", fmt.Errorf("invalid id: %d", id)
    }
    return "result", nil
}

Always return nil for the error when the operation succeeds. Convention: the zero value of error is nil, meaning "no error".

sections.heading: Sentinel errors and wrapping
sections.bodyHtml:
Sentinel errors are package-level variables you compare against:

if err == ErrNotFound {
    fmt.Println("not found, moving on")
}

Add context by wrapping with %w:

if err != nil {
    return fmt.Errorf("fetch user %d: %w", id, err)
}

Unwrap with errors.Is (for sentinel checks) and errors.As (for type checks):

if errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound) { ... }

var netErr *net.DNSError
if errors.As(err, &netErr) { ... }

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/errors/main.go.

Write a function divide(a, b int) (int, error) that returns an error when dividing by zero. Call it from main and print the result or error.

questions.prompt: What type does a Go function return when it can fail?

options.label: The error interface, usually as the last return value
Wraps the error so errors.Is can unwrap it

questions.prompt: How do you check if an error wraps a specific sentinel error?

exercises.title: Safe division
exercises.instructions: Implement divide that returns an error for division by zero. Print results for 10/2 and 5/0.
